title: Quantum-stabilized patterns in a vector Hopfield network
authors: Richard D. Barney, Sharba Bhattacharjee, Victor Galitski, Kartiek Agarwal, Ivar Martin
abstract: We introduce the quantum vector Hopfield network, in which patterns are formed by orientations of quantum vector spins; quantum dynamics arise intrinsically from the non-commutativity of the spin operators. We derive the equations of state and the phase diagrams for this network as well as its classical counterpart. We find that quantum fluctuations, surprisingly, stabilize the stored patterns. Both the critical retrieval temperature and the target pattern overlap are enhanced relative to the classical network. Additionally, we find that this enhancement grows with pattern loading up to network capacity. We interpret this effect as an analog of quantum order-by-disorder, a mechanism by which quantum fluctuations promote the formation of ordered phases. These findings offer a new route to quantum-enhanced associative memory.
summary: This paper analyzes the Quantum Vector Hopfield Network to study associative memory. It theoretically demonstrates that intrinsic quantum fluctuations enhance pattern retrieval stability compared to classical models. The findings suggest a mechanism analogous to quantum order-by-disorder for building robust quantum memories.
main_problem: To investigate how quantum fluctuations affect pattern retrieval stability in associative memory models compared to their classical counterparts.
main_result: Quantum fluctuations surprisingly stabilize stored patterns, enhancing both the critical retrieval temperature and target pattern overlap relative to the classical network. This effect is interpreted as an analog of quantum order-by-disorder.
method: The study uses advanced theoretical techniques including the replica method, free energy calculations, and analyzing corrections ($\Delta f$) due to quantum fluctuations in the Hamiltonian.
model_or_system: The model is the Quantum Vector Hopfield Network (QVHN), a quantization of the classical vector network. Patterns are encoded using continuous unit vectors, and dynamics arise from non-commuting spin operators.
key_observables: Overlap with stored patterns ($m_{\mu}$), connected spin autocorrelation ($q$), Edwards-Anderson order parameter ($q_{EA}$), and free energy density ($f$).
important_parameters: Pattern loading ($\alpha = p/N$), temperature ($T$), and the network capacity limit ($\alpha_r$).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on approximations such as the replica symmetric ansatz, static approximation (time-independent order parameters), and mean-field theory.
figures_summary: Figures compare phase diagrams showing retrieval states are retrievable up to higher loading in QVHN than CVHN; free energy landscapes illustrate quantum fluctuations stabilizing retrieval basins over spin glass minima.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by defining the Hamiltonian and observables, deriving equations of state for both classical and quantum cases using replica methods, comparing stability via free energy analysis, and finally discussing simulation results that confirm quantum stabilization effects.
why_interesting: It provides a theoretical framework showing how fundamental quantum mechanical principles (non-commutativity) can stabilize complex ordered phases in artificial memory systems, offering insights into quantum enhancement of information storage.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) The phase diagram of the QVHN. Memory states are retrievable up to a finite loading capacity of αrN for N spins, and αr ≈0.0508. These states become global minima of the free energy landscape for α < αgr ≈0.0252; hence called the “global retrieval” phase. The quantum network displays greater retrieval transition temperatures than the rescaled classical network (dotted lines). (b) The free energy land- scape for the rescaled classical network at T = 0.6, α = 0.01. Dependence on the parameter rd is removed by enforcing the saddle point constraint corresponding to Eq. (16). The re- trieval minimum sits in a much broader valley than that of the spin glass minimum. (c) Schematic...

FIG. 2. (a) The Mattis magnetization m at several values of pattern loading α for the quantum, classical, and rescaled classical networks. The quantum network displays a less steep initial decrease in m as T increases compared to the classical networks. At T = 0 the Mattis magnetizations of all three networks coincide. (b) The ratios of the quantum critical tem- peratures to the classical critical temperatures for the spin glass, retrieval, and global retrieval transitions. The global retrieval ratio appears to diverge at αgr ≈0.0252 and the retrieval ratio appears to diverge at αr ≈0.0508. The sta- bilizing quantum effects are greatest, relative to the classical network, as the networks...

FIG. 3. (a) The average equilibrium Mattis magnetization m of a state along its initial pattern in a simulated CVHN with N = 200 spins, averaged over 40 network instances and 5 random initializations each. High average m (indicating re- trieval) is seen in the region predicted by the replica analysis, though the transition in α at fixed temperature is seen to be smoother. (b) The average number M of condensed Mattis magnetizations for the simulated equilibrium states at T = 0 as a function of pattern loading α, averaged over 1000 net- work instances and 5 random initializations each. M begins to increase near the value predicted by the replica analysis for the retrieval transition.
